Tafseer:
1. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Ha, Meem. (1)

Like the preceding Surahs, the Qur'an name of this Surah is also 'Ha-Meem'. The sharing of names among the Surahs, as we have pointed out, is proof of the sharing of their contents. Thus, all 'Ha-Meem' Surahs which you have been reading share the same common denominator. If there is any difference, it is in the style of expression, method of reasoning, and brevity or detail.

"By the clear Book," (2)

The Qur'an is Itself a Proof of Its Every Claim

This is an oath sworn by the Qur'an, and its attribute here is mentioned as 'clear' (Al-Mubeen)—meaning the Book which makes things clear; i.e., it is itself a proof of its every claim, standing in no need of any external proof. Those who seek excuses to deny it are trying to throw dust upon the sun, or rather throwing dust into their own eyes.

'The sun has appeared as the proof of the sun.'

Here, the statement sworn to (the maqsam 'alayh) is omitted. In places where the context is entirely clear and the oath itself makes the statement sworn to clear, the statement is omitted. Several examples of this are present in the Holy Qur'an. In Surah Qaf, a highly clear example is present. Here, the word 'mubeen' (clear) has itself pointed to the statement sworn to; because of this, there was no need to mention it separately. As if: 'The sun has appeared as the proof of the sun.'

"Indeed, We have made it an Arabic Qur'an that you might understand." (3)

A Specific Aspect of the Qur'an's Being Clear

This points to a specific aspect of the Qur'an's being clear (mubeen)—showing that We have sent it down in the form of an Arabic Qur'an so that you may understand. This theme has passed in various styles in the preceding Surahs of this group, and we have clarified that sending down the Qur'an in Arabic was both a great favor upon the Arabs and a decisive establishment of proof (itmam al-hujjah). The aspect of favor is entirely clear: God sent down His final and perfect guidance in their language so that they could benefit from it directly, without standing in need of others' teaching, but rather others became indebted to them. The aspect of the establishment of proof is: Allah, by sending down His guidance in their own language, ended their every excuse. Now they cannot present any excuse before God—claiming that the addressees are Arab and the language is foreign!

"And indeed it is, in the Mother of the Book with Us, Exalted and Wise." (4)

The High Pedigree of the Qur'an

This explains the greatness of the Qur'an—showing that it is no plaything or joke, but a highly noble and highly exalted thing. Its high pedigree is explained: it is in the Mother of the Book (Umm-al-Kitab), i.e., the Preserved Tablet (Lawh Mahfudh), which is with Allah, and from there it has been sent down for your guidance. The meaning is: let no one assume it to be the whispering of the Jinn, the fortune-telling of fortune-tellers, the poetry of poets, or the rhetoric of speech-makers, to make fun of it; but this light has descended from that source of light from Whose light alone there is light in the heavens and the earth, and which is the true source of all knowledge. Unfortunate will be those who do not recognize its worth!

The High Status of the Qur'an

Its high status is expressed as: 'Indeed, it is Exalted and Wise' (la-'aliyyun hakeem). This Qur'an is in itself highly exalted and wise. It will be recalled that in the preceding Surah, in verse 51, exactly this same attribute appeared for Allah Almighty, and appeared in the context of the description of revelation and the Qur'an. Obviously, every speech is the reflection of the speaker's attributes. Since Allah Almighty is 'Aliyy' (Exalted) and 'Hakeem' (Wise), His speech is also 'Aliyy' and 'Hakeem'. This hinted: those who possess the capability of recognizing the worth of a gem will value this speech, while the dull and tasteless are neither worthy of it nor will they value it.

By mentioning its high status, the opponents are also directed to the reality: this is the speech sent down by the Creator of the heavens and the earth, not the request of a petitioner. If you do not value it, you will deprive yourselves, harming neither God nor His speech. Its greatness and loftiness are its personal attributes, which are entirely free of need of others' acceptance or rejection.

"Then should We turn the message away from you, disregarding [you], because you are a transgressing people?" (5)

Establishing Proof is Essential Despite Your Disregard

That is: although you are indeed ungrateful and narrow-minded in denying and insulting this exalted and wise speech, and in your choosing of shirk and disbelief to wrong your own souls, you are not ready to reform yourselves, yet this does not mean that you are left to your state, and not fully informed of your diseases and their fatal consequences. Your state, instead of being ignored, requires that you are treated; thus Allah sent down His Book for your warning and reminding. Whether you value it or do not, this reminding will continue until proof is fully established against you—so that whoever has to choose the path of life chooses it with full insight, and whoever has to choose the path of destruction does so after the establishment of proof. This warning and reminding of yours is occurring in accordance with the appointed Sunnah of Allah. No matter how much you reject it out of aversion and arrogance, it will now reach its final consequences.

'Safhan' (disregarding), in my view, is in the accusative of cause (maf'ul lahu) and means overlooking. 'Daraba 'anhu al-shai' means he turned that thing away from him. 'because you are a transgressing people' (an kuntum qawman musrifeen) is the description of their actual disease, and 'musrifeen' here is used in the sense of 'asrafoo 'ala anfusihim'—meaning those who committed excesses to wrong their own souls, and the greatest excess against one's soul is shirk. The meaning is: when you are stained with disbelief and shirk, how was it possible that Allah Almighty withheld His cup of cure from you? The primary ones deserving of medicine are indeed the sick, whether they value it or do not. If you value it, you will do good to yourselves, and if you do not, you invite your own death.

"And how many a prophet We sent among the former peoples, And there came not to them any prophet except that they used to mock him. So We destroyed [nations] stronger than them in power, and the example of the former peoples has passed." (6-8)

Historical Proof in Support of the Preceding Statement

This historical proof is presented in support of the preceding statement, and the address is to the Prophet (pbuh) for his consolation. It states: what your opponents are doing with you today is what the nations of the past did with their respective prophets. Allah Almighty sent many messengers before you with the same objective of warning and reform, but every nation mocked its messenger and insulted his advice. Ultimately, Allah Almighty destroyed them, and those nations were not weak; rather, they were far greater than these people (the Quraysh) in strength and power, but God's punishment broke their backs.

'and the example of the former peoples has passed'—and their examples are present in history. This points to 'Aad, Thamud, and those nations whose destruction has been described in detail in the previous Surahs, and is also coming in the subsequent Surahs.

Explanation of the Polytheists' Contradiction of Thought

"And if you should ask them, 'Who created the heavens and the earth?' they would surely say, 'They were created by the Exalted in Might, the Knowing.'" (9)

This describes in detail that polytheism and stubborn defiance of the Quraysh which has passed in verse 5 with the words: 'because you are a transgressing people'. This description will go far below. It states: although they have stood up to fight with you in defense of their religion of shirk, they are caught in a severe contradiction of thought, to which the heat of their opposition does not let them pay attention. If you ask them: 'Who created the heavens and the earth?' they will necessarily reply: 'Their Creator is the Exalted in Might, the Knowing God.' But on the other hand, their compatibility is: 'and they made of His servants a share' (meaning they set up His partners and equals from among His servants). It should be kept in mind here that the Arab polytheists, as has passed in detail in the previous Surahs, accepted Allah Almighty as the Creator of the heavens and the earth and of everything; but on the other hand, they believed the angels to be the daughters of God who are His beloved ones and His partners in His essence and attributes, and thus their worship is a means of proximity to God and a source of wealth and children.

"He who has made for you the earth a bed and made for you therein roads that perhaps you will be guided," (10)

Four Verses by Way of Inclusion

This verse and the subsequent three verses are not a part of the polytheists' reply, but are included by Allah Almighty to show: whoever reflects on the creation of this universe will find such signs of the Creator's power, lordship, and wisdom within it that they will necessarily accept His Tawhid and also a Day of recompense. The objective of this detail is to show that the polytheists' admission—that the Creator of the heavens and the earth is the Exalted in Might, the Knowing God—contains these requirements which are described below if it proceeds in its correct direction. But the polytheists, after taking the first step correctly, turn to a wrong direction, due to which they invalidate their own accepted belief and lose the straight path.

The Requirements of God's Being the Creator

It states: that same Exalted in Might, Knowing God Who is the Creator of the heavens and the earth (and whose being the Creator is accepted by you as well) made this earth a bed for your living. The further explanation of making it a bed is given in other places in the Qur'an—showing that He, by His great power and wisdom, set up mountains in it so that it does not tilt along with you. Then, He made roads for you in it—meaning He set up mountains to maintain the balance of the earth, but not in such a way that they block your way on all sides; but He made roads between these mountains in both land and sea so that your caravans and your ships can travel from one country to another.

A Highly Significant Clause

'that perhaps you will be guided' (la'allahum yahtadoon) is highly significant and eloquent here. One meaning is: Allah Almighty made roads for you between the towering walls of the mountains in this earth so that you do not remain besieged within these impassable walls, but ways remain open for you to go out. The second highly subtle allusion is: you reflect on your Lord's power, wisdom, lordship, and His endless favors upon you, and reach the conclusion: the Sustainer Who has made this arrangement for you is indeed the deserving recipient of your gratitude, worship, and obedience, and if you do not recognize His right, a Day will necessarily come in which you will have to answer for this ungratefulness.

"And who sends down rain from the sky in measured measure, and We give life thereby to a dead land. Thus will you be brought forth -" (11)

Allah Almighty's Miracle of Lordship

And it is of the miracle of His lordship that He sends down rain from the sky in a measured measure. The rain sending down in a measured measure is clear proof that it is sent down by the determination of the Exalted in Might, the Knowing God Who, under His wisdom, sends down only that much rain which the earth can bear. Then, from this, it also emerged: a single will of the Exalted in Might, Knowing God is operating in both the heavens and the earth. If He had not willed, how was it possible that such a harmony existed between the heavens and the earth—that rain descends from the sky and the earth uses it to bring forth its capabilities? Furthermore, from this, it also emerged: that Exalted in Might, Knowing Being is highly Generous and Caring, Who sends down this rain in a measured measure. With this measure are associated all the blessings of the earth. If any disruption occurs in it, this earth can be ruined by the lack of rain and also by its excess.

Pointing to a Specific Aspect of Rain

'and We give life thereby to a dead land. Thus will you be brought forth'—attention is drawn to this aspect of rain, and since this aspect is what reveals a great reality of this universe, it is described with the first-person plural pronoun, which is proof of special care. It states: you observe that by this water We give life to a dead and dry land, and it flourishes. In the same manner, one day, you will also be raised from this earth after dying and decaying.

"And who created the species, all of them, and has made for you of ships and beasts those which you ride. That you may settle yourselves upon their backs and then remember the favor of your Lord when you have settled upon them and say, 'Exalted is He who has subjected this to us, and we could not [otherwise] have subdued it. And indeed, to our Lord we will return.'" (12-14)

Pointing to Some Other Signs of Power

Referring to some other signs of His lordship, attention is drawn to those requirements which every upright human must feel who is benefiting from them.

It states: He alone is the One Who created various kinds of things for you. 'Azwaj' (species/pairs) here is used in the sense of various kinds and species. In this sense, this word is repeatedly used in the Qur'an and Arabic literature. With 'of every kind in pairs' (min kulli zawjayni-thnayn), this same meaning has been expressed. The variety of things and their being in pairs is in this universe specifically to remind man of the power, wisdom, mercy, and lordship of the Creator. The harmony found within the pairs is presented by the Qur'an as a proof of Tawhid, and its explanation has passed in its proper place.

'and has made for you of ships and beasts those which you ride'—after the general, two specific things are mentioned, showing: He alone is the One Who created ships and such cattle which you ride. During the period of the Qur'an's sending down, these were the well-known سواریاں of land and sea; therefore, they were mentioned. Now, although the list of vehicles has become very long due to the blessing of science, they are all included under these, because the science through which man has invented them has been bestowed by Allah.

The Right of Blessings

'That you may settle yourselves upon their backs...'—this describes the right of these blessings: God gave these سواریاں to you so that you benefit from them and recognize the right of the One Who bestowed them, and when you sit on their backs, remember this favor of your Lord—that without any entitlement on your part, He bestowed these blessings upon you; therefore, it is not suitable for you that you show arrogance on riding them, but at that time, you should supplicate: 'Exalted is He Who has subjected this to us to fulfill our purposes, and we could not have subdued it otherwise.'

In 'That you may settle yourselves upon their backs' (Li-tastaw-oo 'ala dhuhoorihi), although the word 'dhuhoor' (backs) is not highly suitable for ships, as its clear suitability is with horses or other riding beasts, here the word is used by way of dominance (taghleeb). This usage is well-known in Arabic. The objective is to say: whether you ride a ship or a horse, show gratitude to your Lord instead of showing arrogance; but particularly with the mention of horses, this warning was given because the rider of a horse passes before those walking on foot; because of this, the feeling of his superiority (particularly when he is narrow-minded) is stirred up with great intensity, to the extent that like the horse, his own neck also becomes stiff. This very thing has been increased many times in this era by cars. Very few fortunate people sit in cars like gentlemen. Their arrogance is prominent from their every posture, and prominent in such an ugly manner that it becomes difficult to distinguish whether they are human or some other creation!

'Exalted is He who has subjected this to us, and we could not [otherwise] have subdued it'—'Subhan' is a word to declare His purity; i.e., He is pure from every kind of shirk and every small or great blemish. This declaration of purity stirs up the feeling of devotion and submission before God alone, and this feeling protects man from pride, arrogance, rebellion, and corruption, and produces within him lowliness, humility, and gratitude.

'Muqrineen' (capable of subduing) means to gain dominance over one's rival and subdue him. That is: at that time, man must humbly admit before his Lord: 'This is His glory and His favor that He made this mount obedient to us, otherwise we could not have subdued it.'

It should be kept in mind here that this admission is the admission of the actual reality. Whatever is in the service of man in this world is in his service only through the subjection of Allah Almighty; without this subjection, our planning cannot subdue even the smallest thing. You put a nose-string in the nose of a massive animal like a camel, and a rein in the mouth of a horse. If you wish to do the same with the beasts of the forest, after facing a thousand dangers, you cannot ride a lion. It is merely His favor that He created different kinds of beasts for our service and blessed us with the capability to subdue them and use them for our purposes. In this era, the control man has achieved over steam, electricity, and atom has also been achieved through the subjection of Allah. These victories have made man highly arrogant. He has begun to consider himself the master of the universe, whereas Allah Almighty can release them from his control whenever He wills to make them a punishment instead of mercy.

The supplication instructed here relates, in its apparent aspect, to the riding of camels, horses, etc., but this very same supplication is suitable for the advanced vehicles of this era as well—such as cars, airplanes, etc. However, for sea journeys, the more suitable supplication is: 'In the name of Allah is its course and its anchorage' which has been quoted from Hazrat Nuh.

A Highly Significant Clause

'And indeed, to our Lord we will return' (Wa-inna ila Rabbina la-munqaliboon)—just as in verse 10, the words 'that perhaps you will be guided' (la'allahum yahtadoon) were highly significant, these words are highly eloquent, reality-revealing, and shed light on a highly important reality of the philosophy of religion. That is: while riding any vehicle, a person should not remember only that he is going from such and such city to such and such city, but should also remember the reality that one day he must return to his Lord and present himself before Him. The motivation for this is that every blessing is a testimony of His lordship, and lordship requires that the Sustainer gather everyone one day and question them as to how they used His blessings. Then, He rewards those who used them correctly, and takes retribution from those who made them a means of rebellion and corruption. This responsibility and reward and punishment are the logical requirement of this lordship. If this had been absent, this lordship would be meaningless, and this world would be reduced to a plaything of a player. This is an excellent example of moving from one reality to another higher reality, of which several parallels have passed.

"But they have attributed to Him of His servants a portion. Indeed, man is a clearly ungrateful." (15)

Commentary on the Polytheists' Contradiction of Thought

This verse relates to verse 9 ('And if you should ask them, "Who created the heavens..."') above. There, the verses of inclusion appeared after it; because of this, no comment was made on those contradictions which they gathered within themselves. Now, those contradictions are commented upon. The statement is as if: on one hand, their admission is that the Creator of the heavens and the earth is God; on the other hand, they have made some of His servants His portion, i.e., His partners in His essence. This belief of theirs is contrary to their own accepted belief. If Allah Almighty is the Creator of all the heavens and the earth and all that is between them, then everything is His creation, so how can anything be His portion! Any thing's being His portion means that it came into existence from within His essence, and its necessary requirement is that it is also His equal. After accepting this, where remains the oneness and uniqueness of God! It should be kept in mind here that among the polytheists' deities, some were those whom they associated in God's attributes or His rights, and some were those whom they associated in His essence as well. For instance, regarding the angels, their concept was that they are the daughters of God and His beloved ones, and their worship will be a means of intercession and salvation. The Qur'an has refuted their assumption—showing that everything besides God is His creation. No thing has the status of His portion. This is the extreme ungratefulness of man—that he received everything from God, but associates partners with Him to sing their praises and worship them.

If you reflect, this verse also strikes at the belief of Pantheism (Wahdat-ul-Wujood), whose primary originators are Hindu philosophers, but a group of our Sufis has introduced it in Islam as well. According to this belief, the entire universe and its every thing achieve the status of God's portion. So when the Arab polytheists' making of the angels as God's portion was declared shirk, how can making the entire universe as God's portion become Tawhid?

"Or has He taken from what He creates daughters and chosen you for [having] sons? And when one of them is given good tidings of that which he attributes to the Most Merciful as a comparison, his face remains darkened, and he is choked with grief." (16-17)

The Absurdity of the Polytheists' Belief from the Moral Aspect

'Am' (Or) is in the sense of expressing wonder and negation. The polytheists believed the angels to be the daughters of God; He refuted this belief of theirs from another aspect. In the preceding verse, their being His portion was refuted. In this verse, He clarified the absurdity of this belief of theirs from a psychological aspect—showing: 'Not only are you making His creations His portion, but what is worse is that you consider daughters a highly disliked thing for yourselves, yet you feel no shame in attributing them to God. Ask them: when God alone creates everything, why did He choose daughters for Himself when your own condition is that when any of you is given the news of the birth of a daughter, his face turns dark with grief and he remains choked with grief.'

The meaning is: in fabricating this belief, not only did they not utilize their intellect, but this is also the complete negation of that sense of justice which is the requirement of human nature. If they did not understand that God can have no partner, they should have at least shown enough fairness as not to attribute to God what they themselves dislike. In fabricating this belief, they have not only insulted intellect, but have also proved their lack of the sense of justice.

"So is one brought up in ornaments while being in dispute unclear?" (18)

The Comparison of the Arabs' Feeling regarding Daughters during the Jahiliyyah

This is an expression of the feeling they experienced upon hearing the news of a daughter's birth, which became a cause of their grief. It states: they wonder: 'Has she come who is brought up in ornaments and is entirely speechless in the debates of glory?'

'Khasam' (dispute) here is comprehensive of both battle (mubarazah) and boasting (mufakharah), and the Arabs of the Jahiliyyah were devoted to both of these. Among them, wars broke out, and debates of glory were held in which the leaders and poets of every tribe narrated their pride. Obviously, woman was lower in both of these fields. Neither was she a creation of the armor and the sword, nor of eloquence and poetry; because of this, in the eyes of the Arabs, she held no great importance, and this is not specific to the Arabs. In this era as well, the importance she has achieved, she has achieved in the gatherings of display. In terms of battle and boasting, she is still where she was during the Jahiliyyah. It should be kept in mind that this comment on woman is not from Allah Almighty, but is from those Arabs who declared the angels to be the daughters of God. Generally, commentators have assumed that this comment is from Allah Almighty. This misconception occurred because they did not reflect on the context of the discourse.

"And they have made the angels, who are servants of the Most Merciful, females. Did they witness their creation? Their testimony will be recorded, and they will be questioned." (19)

Refuting This Illusion from Another Aspect

He struck at this illusion of theirs from another aspect as well. It states: they have made the angels females, so what is the basis of this claim of theirs—were they present when Allah Almighty created the angels! After this, He threatened in highly severe words: their claim will be recorded, and one day they will be questioned regarding it. This entire discussion on the angels has passed in the commentary of Surah As-Saffat. If required, have a look at it.

"And they say, 'If the Most Merciful had willed, we would not have worshipped them.' They have not of that any knowledge. They are not but falsifying. Or have We given them a book before this, so they are adhering to it? Rather, they say, 'Indeed, we found our fathers upon a religion, and indeed, we are in their footsteps guided.'" (20-22)

The Polytheists' Theological Proof in Support of Their Foolishness, and Its Refutation

This is a reference to that theological proof which the polytheists presented in support of their foolishness—claiming: 'Our worshipping them is itself proof that God approves of their worship. If this had not been pleasing to Him, since His power is competent over everything, He would have stopped us by His willing, and we would not have worshipped them.' In reply, He stated: 'These are their arbitrary statements. They have no knowledge in this matter.' The respite a person receives to commit some evil is no proof of God's approval of that evil. If this were a proof, every thief, adulterer, and corrupt person could present this proof in support of their crimes, claiming: 'If this had been contrary to God's will, He would have stopped us by His willing; but when He did not, it clearly means we did what we did with His approval and He likes our action.'

'Or have We given them a book before this, so they are adhering to it?'—He stated: the source of knowing His approval and His disapproval is His Books and the teachings of His prophets, so have We given them any Book before this Qur'an which they can present as authority? If not, and obviously there is none, then on what authority do they claim that what they are doing enjoys the support of God?

"Rather, they say, 'Indeed, we found our fathers upon a religion, and indeed, we are in their footsteps guided.'" (22)

The Polytheists' Traditional Proof, and Its Refutation

Above, the theological proof of the polytheists was refuted. If you reflect, the polytheists faced exactly the same confusion which our Mujbarah faced. Now, this is a reference to their traditional proof, upon which they relied most, and since its foundation is on blind following (taqleed), which relates to emotions instead of reason, the wicked of every era have used this weapon to incite the emotions of the common people to defeat the efforts of the reformers.

'Ummah' means, as has been explained, the collective way and religion of a nation. It states: these people present in support of their foolishness this proof: 'We found our ancestors upon a correct religion and a high way, and since we are upon their way, we are completely guided. Following their footsteps is our guarantee of guidance. If we deviate from it even slightly, we will lose the path of guidance; therefore, those who are trying to turn us away from this path are seeking our destruction.'

It should be kept in mind that the indefiniteness (tankeer) of 'Ummah' is to express its greatness.

"And similarly, We did not send before you any warner into a city except that its affluent said, 'Indeed, we found our fathers upon a religion, and indeed, we are in their footsteps following.' [The warner] said, 'Even if I brought you better guidance than that [religion] upon which you found your fathers?' They said, 'Indeed, we, in that with which you were sent, are disbelievers.'" (23-24)

The Way of the Deniers of Every Era Has Been the Same

It states: just as these people are blind followers in their religion, they are followers of the previous deniers in their proof. Whichever warner We sent into any city before you, the affluent of the nation replied to his warning and invitation: 'We found our ancestors upon a specific religion, and we will remain firm on following their footsteps.' When the messenger asked them: 'Even if I have brought you a better way than your ancestors' way, will you still remain firm on your ancestral way!' in anger, they replied: 'Indeed, we are deniers of everything with which you have been sent!'

From 'Indeed, we, in that with which you were sent, are disbelievers' (Inna bima ursiltum bihi kafiroon), two matters are clarified:

* First, they gave this reply in anger. This is because the reply goes several steps ahead of the question. The messenger's question was only: 'If my way to which I invite is better than your ancestors' way, will you still remain firm on your stubbornness?' In response, they said: 'We deny your every word.' Meaning, the issue is not of distinguishing between 'better' (ahda) and 'not better'; we are not ready to hear or accept any word of yours.

* Second, the capability to distinguish between 'better' (ahda) and 'not better' has been imbued by Allah Almighty within man, provided he possesses an ear to hear. Provided he is not proud, stubborn, and defiant.

"So We took retribution from them; then look at how was the end of the deniers." (25)

The meaning is: when the nations' stubbornness reached the extent that they refused to listen to and understand the messengers' words, We took retribution from them for their ungratefulness; so see what was the end of those who denied the messengers!

2. Subsequent Theme: Verses 26–45

Next is the reference to Hazrat Ibrahim's (peace be upon him) migration and his declaration of disassociation from shirk, whose primary objective is to clarify: the Quraysh's claim—that the religion of shirk they follow was received by them as an inheritance from their ancestors—is entirely false. Their primary ancestor was Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) who left his nation specifically due to shirk, and this migration of his and his declaration of disassociation remain a firm tradition in both branches of his progeny. Then how do the Quraysh say that they are walking on the path of their ancestors?

After pointing to this historical reality, the actual causes of the Quraysh's rebellion are discussed, and the Prophet (pbuh) is consoled—stating: remain firm on your invitation of truth. This is that very path of the prophets to which you are inviting the people. Allah Almighty could decide between you and your opponents immediately if He willed, but His Sunnah is that He grants respite to the enemies of truth to a specified limit... Recite the verses in this light.

3. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

"And [mention, O Muhammad], when Abraham said to his father and his people, 'Indeed, I am disassociated from that which you worship,'" (26)

Reminder of Hazrat Ibrahim's Declaration of Disassociation

'Bara'' is a verbal noun (masdar) used in the sense of an adjective. When a verbal noun is used in the sense of an adjective, it produces the meaning of exaggeration, like 'Zaydun adlun' (Zayd is justice itself); for this reason, 'Inni bara'' means: 'I am entirely disassociated from you, and no connection remains between me and you.'

This refers to that declaration of disassociation of Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) which has been mentioned in detail in the previous Surahs, and the objective of this reference is, as we have pointed out, to clarify to the Quraysh: in defending their shirk, they refer to their ancestors, so why do they forget their primary ancestor who left his father and his nation specifically due to shirk and settled his progeny in this uncultivable valley! The meaning is: if they are proud of their ancestors' religion, the most glorious ancestor of theirs is Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him), through whose blessing they received the blessings of both religion and the world; so leaving him, why did they adopt the following of those ignorant ones who turned them away from his religion to cast them into the pit of shirk.

Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) made this declaration first before his father, as is clear from: 'when Abraham said to his father'. In this lies a warning to the Quraysh—showing that their ancestor left them a tradition of disassociation from his polytheist father, not of following him blindly.

"'Except Him who created me, and indeed, He will guide me.'" (27)

In my view, this exception is from 'that which you worship' (mima ta'badoon). Just as the Arab polytheists worshipped Allah Almighty but associated partners with Him, Hazrat Ibrahim's (peace be upon him) nation did not deny God but associated partners with Him. Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) clarified to his nation through this declaration: 'The other deities whom you have set up besides Allah are entirely baseless. However, I perform the worship of Allah alone, because He is my Creator; therefore, He deserves to be worshipped.'

'and indeed, He will guide me' relates to his declaration of disassociation—meaning: 'I am making this declaration of leaving my father and my nation, and though this is highly difficult and I am aware of its difficulties, yet I hope from my Lord, for Whose sake I am playing this game, that He will guide me in all difficulties.'

"And he made it a word remaining among his descendants that they might return." (28)

The referent of the object pronoun is that declaration of disassociation and migration mentioned above. Pronouns of this type have passed in the previous Surahs.

The Tradition of This Declaration Remained among the Successors

'a remaining word' (kalimatan baqiyatan) refers to a permanent and remaining tradition. That is, Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him), by his words, deeds, and teachings, made this declaration of disassociation and migration a firm tradition among his descendants. The ancestors continuously advised and instructed their successors to protect and keep this tradition alive. In one branch of his progeny, i.e., the Children of Israel, its mention remained alive through their scriptures and the prophets mبعوث among them. In the other branch, i.e., the Children of Ismail, since no prophets were mبعوث, its mention became weak after a time; however, there always remained a group among them who followed the upright religion of Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him), held his invitation of Tawhid, and protected his tradition of disassociation from shirk.

'that they might return'—meaning he left this tradition among his descendants to serve as a guide for them: whenever Satan tries to mislead them or they deviate, they look at this guide and return to the straight path.

"But, [O Muhammad], I gave enjoyment to these and their fathers until there came to them the truth and a clear messenger. And when the truth came to them, they said, 'This is magic, and indeed we are, in it, disbelievers.'" (29-30)

The Actual Cause of Opposition

'The truth' (Al-Haqq) refers to the Holy Qur'an, and after 'and their fathers' (wa-aba'ahum), the words: 'so the time was prolonged for them, and their hearts became hardened' are omitted due to contextual clarity. In verse 16 of Surah Al-Hadid, in this very context, these words have occurred. Also, in verse 44 of Surah Al-Anbiya, this theme has passed as follows: 'But, [O Muhammad], We gave enjoyment to these and their fathers until life was prolonged for them.'

The meaning is: their claim—that they are opposing the Qur'an because it is contrary to their ancestral religion—is entirely a fabrication; the actual cause of this opposition is that Allah gave them and their fathers enjoyment of the blessings of the world, and a long period has passed on this comfort, which has hardened their hearts. Now, when the Qur'an and the messenger have come to them, this invitation is highly painful to them. They see a danger in its acceptance to their worldly interests; because of this, they call it magic to turn their common people away from it.

The Actual Cause of Calling the Qur'an Magic

We have explained the cause of the Quraysh's calling the Qur'an magic in another place. Any reality, if presented in correct words, naturally affects hearts. Since the Qur'an is entirely the truth, as is clear from the word 'Haqq', and its style of expression is miraculous, it naturally affected those who were not self-interested and who did not fear, like the leaders of the Quraysh, that its success would threaten their leadership. To protect such people from the influence of the Qur'an, the leaders of the Quraysh could not say that the Qur'an was a foolish and meaningless book. If they had said so, their common people would have declared them foolish—showing that they were trying to throw dust upon the sun; however, they tried to convince their common people: 'this eloquence of the Qur'an is not because it is a divine revelation, as its presenter claims, but this is merely the magic of words, in which its presenter is highly expert like our great poets and speech-makers.' As if their effort was to prevent people from believing that the Qur'an is a divine revelation, and to make them treat it in the same category as they treated their great poets' and speech-makers' discourse.

"And they said, 'Why was this Qur'an not sent down upon a great man from [one of] the two cities?'" (31)

Another Slander to Deceive the Common People

To turn their common people away from the Qur'an and the Prophet (pbuh), the leaders of the Quraysh also said: 'If this Book is sent down by God, why was it not sent down upon some great chief of Makkah or Ta'if!' 'The two cities' (Al-Qaryatayn) refers to Makkah and Ta'if, because these two cities were the centers of the chiefs and leaders of Arabia. For generations, the leadership of Arabia belonged to these people; because of this, the simple-minded common people could easily be convinced that in reality, if Allah Almighty had to send down anything for the guidance of people, He would send it down upon some prominent chief of these two cities, and leaving them, why would He choose a person who is poor, whose words the rich are not ready to hear.

"Do they distribute the mercy of your Lord? It is We who have apportioned among them their livelihood in the life of this world and have raised some of them above others in degrees [of rank] that they may take one another in service. But the mercy of your Lord is better than what they accumulate." (32)

Reply to the Objectors

This is a reply to the arrogance of these pharaohs—showing that their statement suggests they consider themselves the sole managers of all the blessings of Allah Almighty. In their view, they have the authority to bless whomever they please and keep whomever they please deprived. Whereas, in this world, those means of livelihood which they have received have been received through the distribution of God. They have not achieved them themselves. If they had achieved them themselves, why would there be a difference in ranks among these wealthy people! Having the matter in one's own choice, why would anyone agree to be lower than another in any aspect? This difference in ranks contrary to their own desire is clear proof that the matter of distribution is not in their hands, but in the hands of another Who, in accordance with His pleasure and His wisdom, extends for whom He pleases and restricts for whom He pleases.

'that they may take one another in service' (liyattakhidha ba'duhum ba'dan sukhriyya): 'ittakhadha fulanun fulanan sukhriyya' means: so and so employed so and so in his work and service.

The Wisdom in the Difference of Ranks

This describes the wisdom: why has Allah Almighty kept a difference in ranks intellectually and materially in this world? He stated: He did so so that people live a life of mutual cooperation, employing one another in their works.

It should be clear that He made this world for trial; because of this, He established its system in such a way that every person is in need of others and also has others in need of him. Even the greatest king is in need of others, and the poorest person has others in need of him. No one is free of need of others, and no one is such that their utility in society is absent. Rather, the Creator of the universe has created every person with different capabilities, different tastes, different levels of intelligence, and different resources. This difference in ranks structures society in such a way that on one hand, great scholars, prominent authors, unparalleled researchers, famous statesmen, and powerful rulers are born in it, and on the other hand, workers in fields and factories, porters, servants, and sweepers are also born in it. All these classes are necessary and indispensable for the structure of society. The nature of their service is different, but none of these elements is contemptible, nor can any be ignored. Rather, to run the machine of society, caring for the smallest part of this machine is also necessary, in proportion to its utility.

By creating the world with this difference of ranks, Allah Almighty is testing: how do those who have been entrusted with high capabilities and better resources use them? Upon receiving them, have they fallen into pride, self-will, and disobedience of the Creator, or are they grateful and obedient to Him, and compassionate to His creation? Similarly, He is also watching those who have been created with lower resources: do they recognize their duties in their sphere of work, fear their Creator, and protect their self-respect, or have they fallen into the drift of bringing down those who are their rulers and officers?

If the first form occurs, through the upright cooperation of the higher and the lower, an upright society and an upright civilization come into existence, and all its components—without any distinction between higher and lower—achieve honor in this world, and in the Hereafter, everyone will receive reward in accordance with their services and sincerity. If the second form occurs, the system of society gradually declines and ultimately perishes, and in the Hereafter too, all its great and small components will deserve God's punishment in accordance with their mischief or negligence.

In this era, those who are in the delusion that they will completely eliminate the existence of classes from the world can never succeed in this until they succeed in making people identical in terms of their intellectual, moral, physical, and practical capabilities, and this is impossible. Those nations who fell into this delusion and shed rivers of blood, their state too is such that major pharaohs exist there, as do those who polish their boots and sweep their streets; and if they did succeed in making the entire nation identical in capabilities, on that very day, the foundation of mutual cooperation would be destroyed, and anarchy would spread in the nation. When every person achieves the capability to become a Lenin or Stalin, why would he agree to become their driver or shoeblack? Then every person will try to become a god, and in the conflict of so many gods, who can estimate the fate of this world!

"And if it were not that the people would become one nation [of disbelievers], We would have made for those who disbelieve in the Most Merciful - for their houses - ceilings of silver and stairways upon which they mount. And for their houses - doors and thrones upon which they recline," (33-34)

An Example of Omission

Here, the words 'of silver' (min fiddatin), just as they occurred after 'ceilings' (suqufan), must also occur after 'stairways' (ma'arij), 'doors' (abwaban), and 'thrones' (sururan); but according to the well-known rules of eloquent Arabic, they are omitted because the context makes them clear. We have opened them in the translation.

The Insignificance of the Enjoyment of the World

Now, the insignificance of the enjoyment of the world is clarified—which led the disbelievers into the pride: 'when all the glory and power of this world has been achieved by us, how is it possible that if God had to send down a Book, He would seek anyone besides us!'

It states: that worldly provision of which they are proud holds no value in the eyes of God. If it were not that people would walk on a single path of disbelief, Allah Almighty would have made for those who disbelieve the ceilings of their houses and the stairways to mount upon them, and the doors of their houses, and the thrones to recline upon, all of silver; but He did not do so because this trial would be highly severe for the people. When the common people saw that those who disbelieve enjoyed this, they would blindly choose the path of disbelief. Only the highly fortunate would protect their faith in this fitnah. To protect the creation from this fitnah, Allah Almighty has given a share in this world to both believers and disbelievers, but in the Hereafter, the disbelievers have no share.

The statement: 'people would become one nation' is said as a general rule, in terms of the majority, not as a strict absolute. Obviously, if the world had been given only to the disbelievers, very few people would have chosen the path of faith, yet some would have certainly chosen it, no matter how small their number would be. Allah has made human nature such that in the most severe trials, such upright ones emerge who pass through oceans of fire but do not leave their Lord.

"And gold ornament. But all that is not but the enjoyment of worldly life. And the Hereafter with your Lord is for the righteous." (35)

The Grammatical Analysis of 'Zukhruf'

'Zukhruf' means ornament, and also gold which is a source of ornament. In terms of construction, it can be conjoined with 'min fiddatin', or we can assume an omitted verb—meaning: 'We would have made for those who disbelieve gold ornaments' or 'We would have piled up gold for them'; there will be no great difference in meaning. That is: if We willed, We could make all these things of gold for them, or pile up gold for them.

Analysis of 'Lamma'

'Lamma' here is not in the sense of 'illa' (except); but it stands in place of that 'la' (indeed) which comes to distinguish between the lightened 'an' (mukhaffafah) and 'an' of negation. In some places, this 'la' is made 'lamma' to fill the phonetic gap in the sentence. For example, in Surah At-Tariq: 'Indeed, every soul has a guardian over it.' (4). Originally, this is: 'In kullu nafsin la-'alayha hafidh', but a phonetic gap remained in the sentence; therefore, to correct the rhythm, in accordance with the usage of the eloquent, it was made 'lamma'. Examples of such additions in letters are numerous in Arabic, but there is no room to go into details here.

The meaning is: these things, on which these people are infatuated, are only the temporary enjoyment of this life. The true, permanent blessings will be received in the Hereafter, and the Hereafter is entirely with your Lord, the share only of those who fear God.

"And whoever is blinded from remembrance of the Most Merciful - We appoint for him a devil, and he is to him a companion." (36)

The Actual Reality

That is, all these doubts and objections of theirs are merely fabricated. The actual reality is: these people have completely turned away from the remembrance of God, and the Sunnah of Allah is: those who turn away from the remembrance of God, Allah Almighty sets a devil over them who becomes their day-and-night companion; then he does not leave them until he makes them the fuel of Hell.

'Asha 'an as-shai' means to turn away from something. The characteristic of a person's heart is: as long as it is populated by the remembrance of God, Satan finds no way in it; but when man becomes negligent of God, gradually Satan gains control over his heart, and when he gains control, escaping from his clutches is not easy. Even the best reminder and advice which comes before him, Satan fabricates doubts and objections against it to turn him away from it. However, those who keep their hearts populated by the remembrance of their Lord, Satan finds no way to enter, and if ever any negligence gives him an opportunity to enter, he finds no place to stay, but as soon as the servant is warned, Satan has to flee.

"And indeed, they avert them from the way [of guidance] while they think that they are [rightly] guided" (37)

Some Usages of the Language

In the preceding verse, since the pronoun 'man' (whoever) is indefinite, it can refer to singular and plural, and the word 'Satan' is also general—referring to the devils of the Jinn as well as the devils of men; because of this, in the verse under discussion, the pronouns are in the plural. Here, the state of the entire group is described. It states: those who are caught in the hands of Satan in this manner face this end: the devils turn them away from the straight path, but those caught in their traps assume that they are entirely guided.

Here, the diversity of pronouns is also noteworthy. The referent of the pronoun in 'innahum' (indeed they) is the devils, and the subject of 'yahsaboon' (they think) is those caught in the devils' traps. But where the meaning is clear, this diversity of pronouns is no defect. A highly clear example has passed in Surah Yusuf:

'Until when the messengers despaired and their people thought that they had been lied to, Our victory came to them...'

"Until, when he comes to Us, he says [to his companion], 'Oh, I wish there was between me and you the distance of the two easts - and wretched is the companion.'" (38)

That is: in this world, these companions share deep friendship; but when they present themselves before Us in the Hereafter and the end of this friendship appears, the one who fell into the trap of some devil to ruin his Hereafter will send curses upon his companion—saying: 'O, if only there was between me and you the distance of the two easts!'

'and wretched is the companion'—this is an expression of aversion and curse from Allah Almighty upon that bad companion, showing what a wretched companion he proved to be, who ultimately cast his companion into this pit.

A Style of the Language

'The two easts' (Al-Mashriqayn) has been generally interpreted by commentators as the east and the west, but in my view, it is not. In Arabic, sometimes the dual form is brought to express the vastness of both ends of something. It is used to express the vastness of the horizons. In the Qur'an, 'Maghribayn', 'Mashriqayn', 'Mashariq', and 'Magharib' have occurred under this very principle. Its explanation has passed.

"And never will it benefit you this Day, when you have wronged, that you are in the punishment sharing." (39)

'when you have wronged'—meaning: 'when you wronged your own souls in the worldly life'.

When this bickering occurs between the misleading and the misled companions, they will be told by Allah Almighty: 'When you committed injustice in the world to wrong your own souls and did not reflect on its end, what comfort will you achieve today by sending curses on one another? This is not going to free any of you. Now both must necessarily face this punishment, so face it. The opportunity for this bickering was in the world, but there you remained loyal and devoted to one another. The time has passed; now it will not return.'

"Then, can you make the deaf hear, or guide the blind and him who is in clear error?" (40)

Consolation for the Prophet

This consoled the Prophet (pbuh)—stating: 'Your warning and advice can be effective on those within whom the capability to see, hear, and understand is alive; how can you make those hear whose ears are deaf, and who have blinded their eyes!'

'and him who is in clear error'—meaning: if someone is in deviation due to the hidden nature of some reality or due to their ignorance, its removal can be planned, but who can guide the one who is in clear deviation, whose being in deviation is clear even to themselves.

"And whether We take you away, indeed, We will take retribution from them, Or We show you that which We promised them; for indeed, We are over them competent." (41-42)

This also relates to the consolation. It states: leave these wrongdoers to their state. Now, either We will take you away from this world and then take retribution from them, or that punishment of which We are warning them will come during your life, and you will see their end. We are competent over punishing them. This theme has passed in verse 46 of Surah Yunus and verse 40 of Surah Ar-Ra'd. Have a look at them if required.

"So adhere to that which is revealed to you. Indeed, you are on a straight path." (43)

That is, in opposition to their nonsense, remain firm on this invitation of Tawhid and this Book which has been revealed to you; you are on the straight path. 'A straight path' (Sirat-e-Mustaqeem) refers specifically to that invitation of Tawhid which has been given in this Surah. The meaning is: your opponents have no proof, they are the flag-bearers of a baseless claim, and you are on a firm foundation, so remain firm on your stance; your opponents' feet will soon be uprooted.

"And indeed, it is a reminder for you and for your people, and you are going to be questioned." (44)

The Responsibility of the Messenger and His Nation

That is, this Qur'an which has been revealed to you is a reminder for you and for your people, and you are all going to be questioned. You will be questioned: 'Did you deliver the revelation to the people precisely and without any deficiency, and what reply did you receive from the nation?' and the nation will be questioned: 'Did there not come to you a warner, such that you have brought this ruin!' In Surah Al-A'raf, it has been described as: 

'Then We will surely question those to whom [a messenger] was sent, and We will surely question the messengers. Then We will surely relate [their story] to them with knowledge, and We were not [ever] absent.' (Al-A'raf: 6-7).

The questioning of the messengers and their nations has occurred in various places in the Qur'an.

The messengers will be questioned regarding their duty of notification, and their nations will be questioned: what appreciation did they show of this great favor of the messenger's advent. Then, the reward and honor which the messengers and their companions deserve will be given to them, and the punishment which their deniers deserve will be their share. In this lies consolation for the Prophet (pbuh) and his companions, and a threat to the opponents—showing that the matter is not going to end here, but this entire case will be presented in the court of God, and there it will be known who won and who lost.

"And ask those We sent before you of Our messengers whether We have made besides the Most Merciful deities to be worshipped." (45)

This refutes the polytheists' claim that the worship of those they worship has been commanded by God—stating: the source of knowing God's commands and prohibitions are His messengers, so ask the messengers who came before you whether God appointed other deities to be worshipped besides the Most Merciful?

'And ask those We sent...' (Was'al man arsalna) is an eloquent style of expression. Obviously, 'messengers' (rusul) refers to their scriptures and teachings which are the representatives of their invitation. This style of expression has been used in previous scriptures, and highly eloquent examples are also found in the speeches of high-class speakers.

4. Subsequent Theme: Verses 46–56

Next, the story of Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) and Pharaoh is described briefly, whose primary objective is to present historical proof for that retribution of Allah which was mentioned in verses 41-42 above—showing that after the nation's denial, the decision regarding them is necessarily made, whether during the messenger's life, after his migration, or after his death. This retribution is His appointed Sunnah, from whose grasp no nation has escaped. Even a giant like Pharaoh, when he fell under its grasp, was drowned along with all his armies. His power was of no use to him.

Second, this is an example of that reality which was mentioned in verse 40—stating that those who destroy their capability to understand and choose deviation deliberately cannot be guided by any sign. They remain blind even after seeing the greatest miracles. Their eyes open only when they see His punishment.

Third, this is a testimony to that reality which was mentioned in verse 45—showing that all messengers sent by Allah brought the very same invitation of Tawhid which the Qur'an is presenting. No messenger ever invited to the worship of anyone besides Allah Almighty... Recite the verses in this light.

5. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

'And We certainly sent Moses with Our signs to Pharaoh and his establishment, and he said, "Indeed, I am the messenger of the Lord of the worlds."' (46)

'Signs' (ayat) refers to those miracles with which Allah Almighty armed Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) to send him to Pharaoh—meaning the staff, the white hand, etc.

'Establishment' (mala') refers to those chiefs and leaders of Pharaoh's nation who were present in his court. Their explanation has passed in the previous Surahs.

'and he said, "Indeed, I am the messenger of the Lord of the worlds"'—this is the invitation which Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) presented to Pharaoh and his chiefs. Here, only a brief reference is made, but other places contain the detail: he informed Pharaoh and his chiefs that he was sent as a messenger of God to warn them, and that Allah had armed him with His signs, and His command was to let the Children of Israel go with him to perform worship in the wilderness.

"But when he brought them Our signs, at once they laughed at them." (47)

Some words are omitted here due to contextual clarity—implying: when Pharaoh and his chiefs heard that Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) claimed to be His messenger and possessed some signs in support of his claim, they demanded to see the signs. Consequently, Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) showed them the signs, but instead of accepting them, they mocked them—claiming: 'What are these signs, these are only the tricks of magic, and we also have magicians who can show greater tricks.'

"And We showed them not a sign except that it was greater than its sister, and We seized them with punishment that perhaps they would return." (48)

Before 'We showed them' (nareehim), an auxiliary verb is omitted according to the well-known rules of Arabic—meaning: 'We continuously showed them signs, each of which was greater than its sister.' This points to those signs which were sent down as warning punishments after the denial of the first signs, so that the Egyptians were warned of His grasp and turned to repentance. These signs appeared one after another in the form of different punishments, and naturally, every sign appeared in a more warning form than the preceding. But such was the hardness of their hearts that no sign was of any use, until they fell under the decisive punishment.

"And they said, 'O magician, invoke for us your Lord by what He has covenanted with you. Indeed, we will then be guided.' But when We removed from them the punishment, at once they broke their word." (49-50)

Whenever any punishment seized them, they ran to Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) and requested humbly: 'Pray to your Lord for us to remove this punishment. If this punishment is removed, we will choose the correct path and follow your word.' But as soon as Allah Almighty removed the punishment, they broke their word and persisted on their stubbornness.

The details of these warning punishments have passed in verses 133-135 of Surah Al-A'raf. We have explained them in the light of the Torah. We present the verses here as well. For details, see their commentary in *Tadabbur-i-Qur'an*:

'So We sent upon them the flood and locusts and lice and frogs and blood as distinct signs, but they were arrogant and were a criminal people. And when the punishment befell them, they said, "O Moses, invoke for us your Lord by what He has promised you. If you [can] remove the punishment from us, we will surely believe you, and we will send with you the Children of Israel." But when We removed the punishment from them until a term which they were to reach, at once they broke their word.' (Al-A'raf).

"And Pharaoh called out among his people; he said, 'O my people, does not the kingdom of Egypt belong to me, and these rivers flowing beneath me? Then do you not see?'" (51)

Pharaoh called out among his people—meaning he had it proclaimed among his people.

Pharaoh's Claim of Superiority over Hazrat Musa

Have a look at verses 31-35 above, which quoted the statement of the leaders of the Quraysh: 'Why was this Qur'an not sent down upon a great man from [one of] the two cities?' Similarly, when Pharaoh saw that the people's devotion to him was being shaken by the proofs and miracles of Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him), he had it proclaimed: 'The sovereignty of Egypt and its flowing rivers are in my control, so how is it possible that if God had to send a messenger, He would send anyone besides me.'

"'Or am I [not] better than this one who is insignificant and can hardly express himself clearly?'" (52)

In this verse, a part of the discourse is omitted. If it is spelled out, the complete statement is: 'When the sovereignty of Egypt and its rivers are in my control, am I better or is this person who belongs to a enslaved nation and can hardly express himself clearly?' Meaning: 'When the situation is such, what was there in this person that God made him His messenger?'

'insignificant' (maheen) points to the fact that Musa (peace be upon him) belonged to an enslaved nation, and 'can hardly express himself clearly' (la yakadu yubeen) points to his lack of eloquence. Under 'And untie the knot from my tongue' (Ta-Ha: 27), we have clarified that Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) was not afflicted with any lisp, as people have assumed, but he was not an eloquent speech-maker, and in that era, to become prominent in public, it was necessary to excel in magic, poetry, or eloquence. Pharaoh criticized this weakness of his—asking: 'On one hand, he belongs to an enslaved nation, and on the other, lacks eloquence, so how can he claim superiority over me?'

"'Then why have there not been placed upon him bracelets of gold or come with him the angels conjoined [in company]?'" (53)

That is: 'According to his assumption, if there is a god and He has sent him as His messenger, then bracelets of gold must have been sent down upon him for his adornment, and the angels must have walked in his company in rows; but he claims to be a messenger of God, and his poverty is before everyone. Reflect on whether a messenger of God would come in this state!'

It should be kept in mind that in that era, kings—particularly the kings of Egypt and Persia—wore gold bracelets to show their glory, and walking in the company of military troops was also an arrangement for expressing their power.

"So he fooled his people, and they obeyed him. Indeed, they were a people defiantly disobedient." (54)

'Istakhaffa' is the opposite of 'istathqala'. 'Istathqala' means to consider something heavy, weighty, and significant; therefore, 'istakhaffa' means to consider someone light, insignificant, and of no worth. 'So he fooled his people' means he found his people simple-minded and fooled them with his deceptive words, and they blindly fell for his deceptions.

Weight is produced within a person by faith.

'Indeed, they were a people defiantly disobedient'—meaning these people were rebellious of God and devoid of His faith; because of this, they were entirely light and insignificant. Such people easily fall into the hands of the devils, and the devils lead them wherever they please. Weight is produced within a person by connection with Allah Almighty. If this weight is absent, his status is like dry wood, which can be blown away by a minor wind.

"So when they angered Us, We took retribution from them and drowned them all," (55)

'Asafoona' means 'aghdhaboona' (they angered Us). It states: when they angered Us with these actions of theirs, We took retribution from them and drowned them all. It should be kept in mind that Pharaoh pursued Hazrat Musa with all his military strength and all his chiefs; because of this, the punishment enveloped his entire army.

"And We made them a precedent and an example for the later generations." (56)

Warning to the Quraysh

'Salaf' primarily means to pass. It is used for those who passed in the past. It can be good and bad. Here, it is in a bad sense—meaning We wiped them out in such a way that they remained only as a story of the past. This same reality has occurred with the words 'We made them narrations' (ja'alnahum ahadeeth) in other places. This is a warning to the Quraysh—showing: if you adopt their way, your name will also be wiped out from the present, and you will remain only as a story of the past.

'And We made them a precedent and an example for the later generations'—'mathalan' means an example and precedent. It can be good and bad. Here, it is in a bad sense—meaning We made them an example of warning for others so that they take warning from the fate of those who showed arrogance against God.

7. Subsequent Theme: Verses 57–65

With the same objective for which the stories of Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) and Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) were described above, the story of Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him) is also pointed to briefly—showing that he also invited to the same Tawhid of Allah, but those who claimed to follow him fabricated innovations to make him the son of God, contrary to his explicit teachings.

The story begins with this introduction: when Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him) is mentioned, the disputers of the Quraysh raise this fitnah: 'This person praises Isa, whereas our deities are better than Isa, because they are angels and the daughters of God, while Isa (peace be upon him) was born of Maryam (peace be upon her).' In this way, they try to convince their common people that the Qur'an's mentioning of him with praise means it accepts him as the son of God and invites to his worship; whereas the Qur'an mentions him as a servant and messenger of Allah, who invited to the same Tawhid to which all prophets invited; but these wicked disputers try to turn even simple matters into fitnah. Recite the verses.

Hazrat Isa's True Position

'He was not but a servant upon whom We bestowed favor, and We made him an example for the Children of Israel'—meaning the Qur'an presents his example not as a deity, but as a servant of Allah upon whom He bestowed His favor, and made him an example for the Children of Israel—showing that the model of the approved life with Allah is this. 'Favor' points to those specific characteristics which have been described in detail in other places in the Qur'an—showing that Allah Almighty created him without a father, by His single word 'Kun' (Be), supported him with the Holy Spirit, made him speak wise words while still in the cradle, and blessed him with the office of prophethood and messengership. He taught wisdom, showed miracles, and He did not let any plot of the Jews succeed against him. To the extent that the Jews' boast—that they crucified him—was refuted by the Qur'an, showing that they did not succeed in this plot either.

An Example for the Children of Israel

'and We made him an example for the Children of Israel'—meaning Allah Almighty made him an example and precedent for the Children of Israel so that they could correct all their ways in the light of this pure model. Every messenger is a divine touchstone for his nation, and in following his model lies the nation's salvation, but Allah Almighty sent Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him) with a specific birth and highly clear miracles because the Children of Israel had forgotten the teachings of all their prophets to become blind and deaf; but they were so hard-hearted that they did not take any warning from this example, as a retribution for which Allah Almighty cursed them forever.

"And if We willed, We could have made of you angels on the earth succeeding [you]." (60)

Removing a Misconception of the Polytheists

Above, the polytheists' statement was quoted: 'Are our gods better or is he?' This is a comment on it—showing that if you have made the angels deities purely because they were created of a higher element (light), this is no proof of divinity. The power of Allah is such: just as He can create the angels of light, He can, if He should will, create from among yourselves people of the attributes of the angels to perform the vicegerency in the earth which has been entrusted to humans; but this is contrary to His wisdom; because of this, He did not do so. The meaning is: a person's birth from a specific element or Hazrat Isa's birth without a father is no proof of divinity or partnership. All these matters are in the power of Allah; therefore, He alone Who is the Creator of this entire universe deserves to be worshipped.

"And indeed, he is a knowledge [i.e., sign] of the Hour, so be not in doubt of it, and follow me. This is a straight path. And let not Satan avert you. Indeed, he is to you a clear enemy." (61-62)

Some Aspects of Hazrat Isa's Being a Sign of the Resurrection

This is said through the tongue of the Prophet (pbuh) to warn the disputers: 'Do not try to make a fitnah out of this. Hazrat Isa is presented not as a deity or the son of God, but as a proof of Tawhid and the Resurrection, so do not doubt the Resurrection, and follow me instead of bickering. This is the correct straight path of life.'

One aspect of Hazrat Isa's (peace be upon him) being a great proof of the Resurrection is: contrary to the general rule, He created him without a father, by His single word 'Kun' (Be). The primary doubt of the Arab polytheists regarding the Resurrection was: how will people be raised alive after dying and decaying? The Qur'an refuted this doubt by showing that in living and dying, the primary factor is not physical causes, but the power and will of Allah Almighty. This is pointed to: the general rule of birth is that a child is born of the relationship of father and mother, but Allah Almighty created Hazrat Isa from the mother alone. In the same manner, He will raise people alive from their graves whenever He wills, and He will face no difficulty in this.

The second aspect is: those miracles that appeared at the hands of Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him) included raising the dead. He raised the dead by the command of Allah, and fashioned the likeness of birds from clay to breathe life into them by the command of Allah. The reference to these miracles is present in the Gospels and also in the Qur'an. Allah Almighty manifested these miracles at his hands because the Jews had fallen into a state of doubt regarding the Resurrection—making them slaves of the world—so that they emerged from it and adopted the path of faith and guidance.

The third aspect is: the manner in which Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him) proclaimed the kingdom of heaven and the eternal life is specifically his share. If one reads his sermons in the Gospels regarding the Hereafter, the heart is filled with the fear and hope of the Hereafter, provided it has not become hard like the Jews' hearts.

Warning the Quraysh through the Prophet's (pbuh) Tongue

'so be not in doubt of it, and follow me. This is a straight path'—meaning: do not doubt the Resurrection, and follow me. This Tawhid to which I invite and the Day of Recompense of which I warn is the correct straight path of life; therefore, your success lies only in following me. In this verse, 'follow me' (wattabi'oon) is clear proof that this was said through the tongue of the Prophet (pbuh). If this had been said directly from Allah, 'follow me' would not be suitable. This word is used in various places in the Qur'an, but is nowhere used for God. Although the thought arises that this was said through the Prophet's (pbuh) tongue, so 'Say' (Qul) or some other similar word must have appeared before it, this is incorrect. There are several examples in the Qur'an showing that within a discourse, some statement is directly made through the tongue of Hazrat Jibreel, the Prophet (pbuh), or some other speaker, and there is no such clarification of who the speaker is. Only the context determines the speaker. Here, the word 'wattabi'oon' (follow me) is clear proof that the speaker is the Prophet (pbuh); because of this, there was no need for clarification.

'And let not Satan avert you. Indeed, he is to you a clear enemy'—this is a part of the warning: never forget that Satan is the clear enemy of this straight path to which I invite. He has clearly challenged Allah Almighty: 'I will surely sit in wait for them on Your straight path.' (Al-A'raf: 16). The meaning is: if despite this warning of mine, you are defeated by an enemy whose enmity is clear, this will be your ultimate misfortune.

"And when Jesus brought clear proofs, he said, 'I have come to you with wisdom and to make clear to you some of that over which you differ, so fear Allah and obey me.'" (63)

The Objective of Hazrat Isa's Advent

This points to the objective of Hazrat Isa's (peace be upon him) advent and his invitation—showing that he came with clear proofs and invited the Children of Israel: 'I have not come to you with a new religion, but with the same religion which Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) brought. However, I have brought the wisdom of religion of which you deprived yourselves, so that faith becomes alive within you, and to make clear to you some of that over which you differ.' The context is proof that 'and to make clear' (wa-liyubayyina) is conjoined with an omitted clause. We have explained it.

It should be clear that Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him) was not the presenter of a new Shariah, but was the confirmer of the Torah; however, he wanted to make the Children of Israel familiar with the wisdom—meaning the spirit and core—of religion; but they did not value it. Rather, they remained caught in literalism, which resulted in the actual religion being lost from within them, and only some rituals remained, by performing which they became satisfied that they had performed all the duties of Allah and His religion.

Wisdom Removes Differences

If the wisdom of religion is lost and only rituals and words remain, its logical consequence is also that differences arise in the religion which cannot be resolved. The Jews also fell into this fitnah after being deprived of the wisdom of religion. Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him) wanted to remove their religious differences through teaching wisdom, but the Jews did not value this wisdom, which resulted in their differences continuously increasing, until they faced that end which was the logical consequence of their disregard.

"Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him. This is a straight path." (64)

Hazrat Isa's Primary Invitation Was Tawhid

Exactly this same theme, with a minor change of a single word, has passed in verse 51 of Surah Al-Imran, where we have explained it in detail. This describes Hazrat Isa's (peace be upon him) primary invitation—showing that like other prophets, he also invited to the worship of Allah alone, not to his own or anyone else's worship. Under the aforementioned verse of Surah Al-Imran, we have clarified: 'my father and your father' which has occurred in the Gospels is expressed by the Qur'an with 'Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord'; the sense in which he called Allah his father was that in which he called Him others' father, and considered Him alone worthy of worship. It has been explained that the word 'Ab' in Hebrew is used in the sense of father and also in the sense of lord, and its meaning is determined by the context.

'This is a straight path'—meaning the straight path to reach God is this alone. If anyone else is associated as His partner, this straight path is deviated or blocked.

"But the factions differed from among them, so woe to those who wronged from the punishment of a painful Day." (65)

The Differences among the Christians

That is, Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him) gave His invitation to Tawhid in highly clear and decisive terms, but different factions among his followers adopted different religions. The nature of these differences has passed in its proper place. After Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him), except for the followers of Simon, all other factions adopted Trinity, atonement, etc., completely ending Hazrat Isa's (peace be upon him) teachings of Tawhid. Modern Christianity is entirely a collection of the innovations of Paul, and they have also divided into different factions in the description of these innovations.

'so woe to those who wronged from the punishment of a painful Day'—'those who wronged' (alladhina zalamu) refers to those who committed shirk to wrong their own souls. It states: for such people is the destruction of a painful Day's punishment!

8. Subsequent Theme: Verses 66–89

Next are the concluding verses of the Surah, in which first the good end of the believers and the bad end of the disbelievers are clarified. After this, consoling the Prophet (pbuh), He instructed him to turn away from those who mock the guidance of Allah and are carefree regarding their end. Finally, Tawhid is reminded again—showing that those who are carefree regarding the Hereafter relying on false intercession should remember that on that Day, everyone will face only the One God, and no one's intercession will be of any use... Recite the verses.

9. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

"Do they await except the Hour that it should come to them unexpectedly while they do not perceive?" (66)

Having described the above proofs, this consoled the Prophet (pbuh)—stating: these people are not listening to your word because they do not value your warning. Their attitude is witness: they want the Resurrection to come upon them unexpectedly in such a way that they do not perceive, instead of being warned and preparing for it. If they want this, leave them to their state. Allah willed to grant them respite before that Day so that they prepared for it, but they want it unexpectedly; therefore, there is no need to worry over them. They themselves will see the end of their carelessness.

"Close friends, that Day, will be enemies to each other, except the righteous." (67)

The State of Self-Concern on the Day of Judgment
