That is, in this world they rely on their friends, helpers, family, and partners, which has made them carefree regarding the Hereafter; but on the Day that is to come, no close relative and no close friend will be of any use to anyone; rather, these friends will become enemies of one another, because their mutual friendship became the cause of this eternal destruction. If they had not agreed with one another, but had reflected on their good and evil with seriousness, they would not have remained deprived of the blessing of the Prophet's word. On that Day, every person will beat their head over this deprivation and reproach their companion, but this regret will be entirely useless. Above, in verses 36–39, this theme has passed in an excellent style.

'except the righteous'—meaning God's believing and pious servants will remain safe from this bad end. They advised one another of truth and justice, and remained firm on it themselves; for this reason, on the Day of Judgment, they will be happy with their success and will welcome their companions with peace and greeting.

"[To them it will be said], 'O My servants, no fear concern you this Day, nor will you grieve - [You] who believed in Our verses and were Muslims. Enter Paradise, you and your kinds, delighted.'" (68-70)

Allah's Greeting to the Pious Servants

This greeting will be given by Allah Almighty to those pious servants: 'O My servants! Now you have emerged from the home of trial of fear and grief to enter this eternal kingdom in which neither will any fear touch you, nor will you grieve.' We have explained in another place: fear is of future dangers, and grief is of present and past failures. Paradise is a place where the believers will remain safe from both; for this reason, this is a highly comprehensive style to express Paradise.

'[You] who believed in Our verses and were Muslims'—this describes those attributes of the pious servants due to which they will deserve this great reward... by describing these attributes, the discourse is reconnected with the situation—as if Allah Almighty pointed to them: 'The ones deserving of this high station will be those who believed in Our verses and are obedient to Our commands today.' This showed automatically: those who claim to believe but do not obey His commands are not deserving of this glad tidings.

Analysis of the Word 'Azwaj'

'Azwaj' is used in the Qur'an in the sense of wives, and also in other meanings—such as various kinds. In Surah Ta-Ha, it is: 'and sent down water from the sky, and We brought forth thereby various species of diverse plants.' (53).

Similarly, it has also occurred in the sense of co-religionists and companions. For example:

'Do not extend your eyes toward that which We have given enjoyment to [various] categories of them...' (Al-Hijr: 88).

'"Gather those who committed injustice and their companions and what they used to worship..."' (As-Saffat: 22).

'And you become [of] three kinds' (Al-Waqi'ah: 7).

In the light of these parallels, our view is: in the verse under discussion, 'azwaj' is used in the sense of co-religionist and companion groups. We have maintained this in the translation. Although the ranks will be different in accordance with their faith and deeds, all believers will be delighted with the blessings of Paradise. The companions of the disbelievers were mentioned above—stating that they will become enemies of one another. In contrast to them, the state of the believers is described—stating that they will be delighted.

"Circulated among them will be plates of gold and cups. And therein is whatever the souls desire and [what] delights the eyes, and you will abide eternally therein." (71)

'Sihas' means plates, and 'akwab' means cups. Just as the word 'gold' (dhahab) occurred with 'sihas', it also occurred with 'akwab', but was omitted to avoid repetition. We have opened it in the translation. That is: the youths of Paradise will circulate before them with plates and cups of gold for their hospitality.

'And therein is whatever the souls desire and [what] delights the eyes'—within these plates and cups will be those foods and drinks which are beloved to hearts and delightful to eyes. Some things are good in taste but do not look good. Allah Almighty's hospitality for the people of Paradise will be with those blessings which are delicious to taste and delightful to eyes.

'and you will abide eternally therein'—the preceding statements were made in the third-person style, and this statement in the second-person style. This change is a proof of special favor—implying: with special care, Allah Almighty will give them glad tidings: 'rest assured, what has been bestowed upon you is no temporary honor, but you will abide eternally in this Paradise.' If there is any fear regarding even the greatest blessing that it is temporary, it ruins all the comfort. For this reason, Allah Almighty will assure the people of Paradise: 'now enjoy this Paradise in comfort, no one can deprive you of it.'

"And that is Paradise which you are made to inherit for what you used to do." (72)

Paradise is Bestowed not Merely as a Gift, but as a Right

Above the preceding glad tidings, Allah Almighty will give them this glad tidings: 'This Paradise has been bestowed upon you in reward for your deeds.' That is: this is not merely a gift, but your right. If some honor is without entitlement, the heart does not experience true joy. Allah Almighty has kept in view this aspect of human nature; because of this, He declared Paradise to be the right of the people of Paradise and the reward for those efforts they bore in the path of truth in the world.

"For you therein is much fruit from which you will eat." (73)

In verse 71, the foods and drinks of the people of Paradise were mentioned. This points to their fruits—stating that for their enjoyment, there will be countless kinds of fruits, from which they will eat as they please, and there will be no fear of any shortage.

"Indeed, the criminals will be in the punishment of Hell, abiding eternally," (74)

Eternal Despair for the People of Hell

Following the description of the reward of the pious, this describes the fate of the criminals—stating that they will remain in the punishment of Hell eternally. They will never achieve release from it.

"It will not be subsided for them, and they will be therein in despair." (75)

This punishment will be so dominant over them that let alone achieving release, it will never be averted or lightened even temporarily. 'and they will be therein in despair' (wa hum feehi mublisoon)—meaning they will be entirely in despair. Even a minor hope of some release or of some reduction in the punishment is sometimes a support for the despairing, but they will have no such support.

"And We did not wrong them, but it was they who were the wrongdoers." (76)

That is, this situation which they will face is no injustice from Allah Almighty, but they prepared its causes themselves; therefore, they became wrongdoers to their own souls. The arrangement necessary for their guidance was made by Allah, but they did not value it, spending all their life in the servitude of desires, which resulted in their facing this end.

"And they will call, 'O Malik, let your Lord put an end to us!' He will say, 'Indeed, you are to remain.'" (77)

The Appeal of the People of Hell to the Keeper of Hell, and His Reply

This explains their eternal despair mentioned in verse 75—stating: they will say to the keeper of Hell: 'O Malik! If there is no room for mercy, pray to your Lord to put an end to us!' He will immediately reply: 'You will not be put an end to; you are to remain in this state.' For the despairing, the final support is death, but they will be deprived of this support as well, and this will be their greatest deprivation.

"We had certainly brought you the truth, but most of you were averse to the truth." (78)

Warning to the Quraysh

Following the description of the fate of the criminals, this is a warning to the Quraysh—stating: We have presented the truth before you in the form of the Qur'an, but most of you are averse to the truth. If you are averse, reflect on the end you are to face. If you face this end, it will be no injustice from Allah Almighty, but you will have wronged yourselves.

"Or have they devised an affair? But indeed, We [also] are devising." (79)

'Ibram' means to make a matter firm. 'Ibrama-l-habl' means to spin the rope firmly. Here, it is used in the sense of making a decisive resolution.

Warning of Decisive Punishment to the Quraysh

This is a threat of a decisive punishment to the Quraysh, and to make it severe, the third-person style is adopted—as if they do not deserve address. It states: if they have made a decisive resolution, We have also made a decisive resolution. That is: if they have resolved to deny the Qur'an and the Prophet, they should remember that We have also resolved to destroy them in accordance with Our Sunnah.

We have been clarifying the Sunnah of Allah regarding the messengers—showing that once the nation resolves to expel or kill the messenger, Allah Almighty grants them no further respite, but saves the messenger and his companions and destroys the nation. This points to that Sunnah: if these people have become united in denying the messenger, they will fall under the Sunnah of Allah, and nothing will save them. This indicates that as long as the nation is in doubt regarding the invitation, He grants them respite; but once they make a decisive resolution to end the caller and the call, the final decision of God also stands manifest.

"Or do they think that We hear not their secrets and their private conversations? Yes, [We do], and Our messengers [i.e., angels] are with them recording." (80)

Pointing to Secret Conversations

Although Allah Almighty knows every secret and private conversation of people, the context indicates that this points specifically to those private conversations which the leaders of the Quraysh held in Dar-al-Nadwah, etc., regarding killing or expelling the Prophet (pbuh). The meaning is: let these people not remain in the misconception that God is unaware of their plots and private conversations. He is fully aware of all their secret activities, and His angels are with them recording every word. As soon as they make their final resolution regarding the Prophet, God's decision regarding them will stand manifest. Then all their plans will remain unfulfilled, and the decision of God will be implemented.

"Say, 'If the Most Merciful had a son, then I would be the first of [his] worshippers.' Exalted is the Lord of the heavens and the earth, Lord of the Throne, above what they describe." (81-82)

A Decisive Declaration Referring to the Initial Theme

At the end, referring to the discussion at the beginning regarding the refutation of the divinity of the angels, He made a decisive declaration—stating: tell these people: 'If it is proven that God has progeny, I would be the first to worship them; but the Lord of the heavens and the earth and the Throne is pure from what these polytheists attribute to Him without any proof.' He alone is the Creator of all these things, the Master of everything, and the sole Ruler of His Throne of sovereignty. He stands in no need of any son, daughter, partner, or assistant.

"So leave them to converse and play until they meet their Day which they are promised." (83)

That is, having made this decisive declaration, leave them to their state. Let them say whatever they please and mock as they please, until they meet that Day of which they are being warned. On that Day, the reality will become manifest to them—showing that those whom they worshipped as God's progeny were of no use to them.

"And it is He who is [the only] deity in the heaven, and on the earth [the only] deity. And He is the Wise, the Knowing." (84)

That is: He alone is the Deity of the heavens, and He alone is the Deity of the earth, and His command and will alone operate in both. Their connection and harmony are proof that they are operating under the will of one Powerful and Self-Sustaining God. If they had different gods, they would have been destroyed.

'And He is the Wise, the Knowing'—He is Wise and Knowing; neither is His any action devoid of wisdom, nor does He stand in need of anyone's help for His knowledge; for this reason, the belief of false intercession, upon whose hope the polytheists sit carelessly regarding the Hereafter, is entirely in vain. This belief is the negation of His wisdom and His knowledge.

"And blessed is He to whom belongs the sovereignty of the heavens and the earth and what is between them and with whom is knowledge of the Hour and to whom you will be returned." (85)

Within 'Tabaraka' (blessed is He) is the aspect of God's greatness and also of His being the source of goodness and blessing. His being Great and Blessed requires that He alone is feared and hope is placed in Him alone. He is the Sovereign of the heavens and the earth and all that is between them; because of this, no one has the audacity to open their mouth before Him or interfere in His will. At the same time, He is of great mercy and blessing; because of this, He will deal with everyone in accordance with the requirements of His mercy. For His mercy, the servants do not stand in need of anyone else's intercession.

'and with whom is knowledge of the Hour and to whom you will be returned'—the exact knowledge of the Hour of Resurrection is with Him alone; if the Prophet (pbuh) cannot tell its exact time, it does not mean it will not come. Its coming is certain, and remember well that you are to return to Him alone, and no one else will be a place of return for you on that Day.

"And those they invoke besides Him do not possess [power of] intercession; but only those who testify to the truth [i.e., believe in Allah's oneness], and they know." (86)

This explains 'and to whom you will be returned'—showing: the polytheists are in the false assumption that their protectors and intercessors will intercede for them to save them from God. It states: those whom they call upon besides Allah do not possess any authority over intercession. On that Day, the decision will be entirely in the control of Allah, and He will judge with complete justice: 'And Allah judges with truth, while those they invoke besides Him do not judge with anything' (Ghafir: 20).

Whoever Testifies Will Testify to the Truth

'but only those who testify to the truth, and they know'—the exception is disconnected (munqati'); meaning: on that Day, no one will possess any authority of intercession, but those whom Allah Almighty permits will testify to the truth, and their testimony will be of what they know. In the Qur'an, it is repeatedly clarified: before God, only those will speak for intercession whom Allah Almighty permits, and will speak only for whom they receive permission. In Surah Ta-Ha, He stated: 'That Day, no intercession will benefit except [that of] one to whom the Most Merciful has given permission and has accepted his word.' (109). Similarly, it is clarified: whoever speaks will speak after His permission, and will say only what is correct. In Surah An-Naba': 'The Day that the Spirit and the angels will stand in rows, they will not speak except one to whom the Most Merciful permits, and he will say what is correct.' (38). Similarly, it is also clarified: the close servants of God will say only what is in their knowledge, and will not dare to speak of what is beyond their knowledge. In verse 117 of Surah Al-Ma'idah, Hazrat Isa's statement has passed: 'And I was a witness over them as long as I was among them'—meaning: 'I have no knowledge of what they fabricated after me; You alone know it.' This shows that the prophets' (peace be upon them) testimony will relate only to the period they spent among their nations. They will say nothing regarding subsequent periods, because they will be unaware of their states.

"And if you should ask them, 'Who created them?' they would surely say, 'Allah.' So how are they turned away?" (87)

This is an expression of wonder over the polytheists—stating that they consider others besides Allah capable of intercession. It states: if you ask them who created them, they will necessarily reply: 'Allah!' After accepting this reality, it is unknown where their intellect is turned upside down that they also assume: their deities enjoy such proximity to God that they can save whomever they please from Him and secure high ranks for whomever they please through their intercession.

"And [Our Messenger] says, 'O my Lord, indeed these are a people who do not believe.'" (88)

The Conjoined Clause of 'Qilihi'

'Qilihi' (his statement) is conjoined, in my view, with 'bil-haqq' (to the truth) in verse 86. That is: they will say only what is correct, and their testimony will be: 'O my Lord, indeed these are a people who do not believe.' The intermediate verse appeared as a parenthetical clause; because of this, no معنوی distance was produced. Precedents of this have passed and will come.

It should be kept in mind here: the assumed partners whom the polytheists present as excuses on the Day of Judgment will reject their excuses right in their faces. For example, regarding the angels, it is clarified: Allah Almighty will ask them whether these polytheists used to worship them? The angels will declare their disassociation in reply—stating that these people carrying the responsibility of their deviation, and they have no connection with their deviation:

'And [remember] the Day He will gather them all and then say to the angels, "Did these people use to worship you?" They will say, "Exalted are You! You are our benefactor, not them. Rather, they used to worship the jinn; most of them were believers in them."' (Saba: 40-41).

Hazrat Isa's (peace be upon him) testimony is mentioned in these words:

'And [remember] when Allah will say, "O Jesus, Son of Mary, did you say to the people, 'Take me and my mother as deities besides Allah?'" He will say, "Exalted are You! It was not for me to say that to which I have no right. If I had said it, You would have known it..."' (Al-Ma'idah: 116).

This same reality is clarified in Surah Al-Ahqaf as follows:

'And who is more astray than he who invokes besides Allah those who will not respond to him until the Day of Resurrection, and they, of their invocation, are unaware. And when the people are gathered [that Day], they [i.e., the deities] will be [rendered] enemies to them and will be deniers of their worship.' (Al-Ahqaf: 5-6).

"So turn aside from them and say, 'Peace.' But they are going to know." (89)

This instructed the Prophet (pbuh): if these people are bent upon seeing their final end, leave them to their state, and turn aside from them exactly as Ibrahim (peace be upon him) turned aside from his father. 'Salam' (peace) here is in the sense of departure. 'But they are going to know'—meaning there is no great delay in the manifestation of that end which they are awaiting. They will soon see it.

By the grace of Allah, the commentary of this Surah is completed on these lines. Praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds.
